imageCaption: The butterfly leads deeper into a garden where flowers bloom in reverse sequence, their petals drawing backward into buds before dissipating into seeds, while the family follows paths they haven't chosen yet.
text:
The butterfly didn't fly like normal butterflies.

Normal butterflies moved through air like they were negotiating with it—a kind of probabilistic zigzag that could never quite decide which direction was actually interesting. This butterfly moved like it had already seen where it was going. Like every decision it made had been made before and all it had to do was let itself follow the pattern it had already chosen.

Aaliyah kept expecting to lose sight of it. The garden was large, and getting larger in ways that didn't make architectural sense. She'd stepped through the yellow doors exactly thirty seconds ago and was already fairly certain that the garden had more paths than a family of four plus one very enthusiastic dog could theoretically navigate in a single afternoon.

"It's doing something," Elijah announced. He'd positioned himself two steps ahead of everyone, the way he always did when he was reading a situation that only he seemed to understand. "The butterfly is organizing things. Look at the flowers."

Aaliyah forced herself to actually see what was happening instead of just watching the butterfly. The garden was in bloom—except not in any forward-moving sense. The flowers she was passing were existing in what looked like temporal reverse. A rose that had been full-petaled at the garden's edge gradually lost its petals as she moved deeper. A tulip seemed to compress itself toward a bud-state. A bloom that might have been a peony was actively becoming less alive as they walked past it.

"It's showing us what we left behind," Isla said. She was moving beside them, not ahead or behind but somehow consistently at the exact position where you'd want to find her if you suddenly needed someone to explain impossible things. "Not metaphorically. Literally. The flowers are existing in reverse-temporal sequence. You're moving backward through a garden that's already bloomed."

"That doesn't sound peaceful," Luisana said, but there was no fear in her voice. She'd moved past fear about three weeks ago when she'd accepted that her family's life was never going to make sense in traditional ways and that this was probably ideal. "That sounds like the opposite of growth."

"It's preparation," Isla replied. "You're not destroying the flowers by moving through their past versions. You're learning what it looks like to undo growth. To return things to their potential state. Because that's what you've been doing with the glitch zones. You've been undoing what went wrong so that the potential for rightness could exist again."

The butterfly had landed on a flower—or possibly a flower had decided to become a landing place. The distinction was unclear. The insect seemed to glow against the petals, its iridescent blue so vivid against the pale pink of whatever bloom it had chosen that Aaliyah had to look away.

"Is this a glitch?" Elijah asked cheerfully. "Is the garden a glitch zone?"

"No," Isla said. "The garden is the place where glitch zones come to heal. It's where time goes to remember why it wanted to move forward in the first place. Most families never see it. Most families don't generate enough emotional resonance to accidentally heal temporal fractures and reach backward through time to do it."

Anthony was staring at the un-blooming flowers with an expression of deep concentration. He had the pegboard tucked under his arm—he'd taken to carrying it everywhere after the Madrid-1990 plate incident. As if the collection of miniature plates had become something that needed protection.

"So what does it want?" he asked suddenly. "The garden. If it's a place where glitch zones heal, what's it trying to show us by being in reverse-bloom?"

"What do you want it to show you?" Isla countered.

Anthony's eyes narrowed. This was his analytical face—the one he used when he was trying to solve problems that didn't have vacation-booking solutions. "It's showing us the moment before growth happens. It's showing us that every flower has a second when it's not yet bloomed. When it's all potential."

"Yes."

"And we're at that moment," Anthony continued. "For us. As a family. We've healed everything that went wrong. We've reached backward and fixed what the Cascade broke. But we haven't decided what we're building yet. We're all potential. We're all about to bloom and we're standing in the garden staring at our own reverse moment."

Luisana made a sound—not quite crying, not quite laughing. The sound someone makes when they understand something too big to fit into words. She reached over and took Anthony's free hand. The pegboard dangled between them, holding forty-three memories of everywhere they'd been.

The butterfly lifted off from its landing place. This time, instead of moving deeper into the garden, it moved backward—toward the yellow doors they'd entered through. Toward the house. Toward the path they'd already traveled.

"It's showing you the way home," Isla said softly.

"But we don't have a home," Elijah said, not sadly, just as a statement of fact. "We have a car and we have Sully and we have each other but we don't have a house with a door and a number on it."

"Not yet," Isla said.

She was looking directly at Anthony when she said it. At Anthony and his collection of miniature plates. At Anthony and the photographs Luisana had taken of Curaçao real estate listings at 2 AM when she thought everyone was asleep. At Anthony and the kind of man who could book a flight to "somewhere with a beach, probably" and accidentally heal the entire timeline just by being authentically, chaotically himself.

"You're saying—" Luisana started.

"I'm not saying anything," Isla interrupted. "You're saying it. You've been saying it for seventy days. Both of you. Every time you look at the same thing and understand it's what you want without having planned it. Every time your daughter maps a glitch and realizes she's actually mapping the shape of your love. Every time your son asks a question that's too innocent and too true. Every time your dog wags when spontaneity happens."

The butterfly had reached the threshold of the garden. It paused at the yellow doors, wings catching the light one final time before disappearing inside the house.

"She's waiting for you to follow," Isla said. "Not just into the house. All the way through it. To whatever comes next."

Sully, who had been very content to walk backward through a temporally reversed garden and ask zero questions about it, suddenly perked up his ears. Not in the direction of the butterfly. In a different direction entirely. Deeper into the garden, where the flowers hadn't just stopped blooming—they seemed to have stopped existing entirely.

His bark was different than the warning barks Elijah had codified. Not three short ones. Not one long one. Something else entirely.

"What's that?" Aaliyah asked.

"That," Isla said, her voice suddenly sharp, "is a bark I haven't heard before. That might be important."

And then the garden shifted.

Not violently. But completely. The reverse-blooming flowers froze mid-unblooming. The paths suddenly had edges. And in the distance, where there had been nothing a moment ago, a door appeared. Not yellow. Not anything in the visible spectrum at all. A door that looked like it was made of possibility itself.

"Oh," Elijah said softly. "There's one more thing. There's always one more thing we didn't know about, isn't there?"

"Yes," Isla said. "And that's your next glitch zone. And that door is where you need to go. And I can't follow you through it."

Sully barked again. The same impossible bark.

And the butterfly, from somewhere inside the house, seemed to shine a little brighter.

Aaliyah looked at her family. At her father holding his collection of places they'd been. At her mother's hand in his. At her brother who understood things that made no sense until they made all the sense in the world. At her dog who was reading an impossible door like it was a message written specifically for him.

"Ready?" Anthony asked.

No one said yes. But no one said no either. And that, Aaliyah was learning, was the only kind of ready that actually mattered.
